characters.relation: Twin brother; slain at the founding
characters.blurb: Twin of Romulus. Captured while raiding Amulius’s herds, then killed—by Romulus or by Celer—when he mocked the new walls.

characters.relation: Maternal grandfather; rightful king of Alba
characters.blurb: Deposed by Amulius, he recognized the twins and with them recovered Alba Longa before they founded Rome.
Seized Numitor’s throne, forced Rhea Silvia into Vestal vows, and ordered the twins drowned. They returned and killed him.
Daughter of Numitor, made a Vestal by Amulius. Mars (or a lover) fathered Romulus and Remus upon her.
War-god said to have fathered the twins on Rhea Silvia—the pedigree Rome claimed for its martial destiny.
Royal herdsman who found the twins at the fig-tree and raised them with Larentia. He later revealed their birth to Numitor.
Wife of Faustulus who nursed the twins. Some say “Lupa” was her nickname as a courtesan; others keep the wolf literal.
King of the Sabines who warred on Rome after the rape of the women, then shared the throne with Romulus until he was murdered at Lavinium.
Chief among the stolen Sabine women. She begged peace between fathers and husbands and later was said to share Romulus’s divine honors.
Daughter of the citadel’s commander; she opened the gate to Tatius for what the Sabines wore on their left arms—and was crushed under their shields.
Father of Tarpeia; entrusted with the Capitoline when the Sabines attacked. His daughter’s treachery cost Rome the rock.

characters.relation: King of Caenina; first spolia opima
characters.blurb: Ruler of Caenina who challenged Romulus after the rape. Romulus killed him and dedicated the first spolia opima to Jupiter Feretrius.

characters.relation: Wall-guard who may have killed Remus
characters.blurb: One tradition makes this swift lieutenant, not Romulus, the man who struck Remus for leaping the unfinished wall.

characters.relation: Early Sabine war casualty; ancestor of Tullus
characters.blurb: Roman who fell fighting the Sabines; his grandson Tullus Hostilius later became king. Plutarch marks the family among the first mixed houses.

characters.relation: Trojan ancestor of the Alban line
characters.blurb: Trojan exile whose Italian settlement founded the line of Alba Longa—Numitor, Rhea Silvia, and so the twins.
Arcadian who held the Palatine before Romulus; his rites of Hercules and the Lupercal color Rome’s oldest cults.

characters.relation: Hero honored at the Ara Maxima
characters.blurb: Evander’s guest-hero whose altar Romulus’s Rome kept. His cult marks the Greek strand in early Roman religion.
The god Romulus became when he disappeared in the storm—or so Proculus Julius swore to the mourning people.
Patrician who swore Romulus met him on the road as Quirinus and ordered the Romans to cultivate arms and know their city would rule.
Companion (or cry) tied to the rape of the Sabine women; Romans later shouted “Talasio” at marriages as a lucky omen.
In one strange variant, an Alban king whose hearth-phallus story rivals the Mars-and-Rhea birth of the twins.
Wealthy man married to a handmaid in the alternate birth story Tarchetius’s court produced.
Latin town Romulus seized after it stirred against Rome—one of the early wars that widened his territory.
People of Veii who fought Romulus after Fidenae fell. Their wars foreshadow Camillus’s later siege.
Learned king whose antiquarian notes Plutarch quotes among conflicting accounts of Rome’s beginnings.
Marcus Terentius Varro, whose chronology and lore Plutarch weighs against other founders’ dates.
